title: AI and the Systemic Restructuring of Work

# AI and the Systemic Restructuring of Work

The claim that "AI won't take your job, but someone using AI will" has become a kind of mantra in discussions of technological displacement. It sounds actionable and empowering. It is also misleading in a way that matters, because it locates AI's impact at the wrong level of analysis. The real transformation is not happening at the level of individual tasks or tools but at the level of systems — how work is organized, how value flows through economies, how roles are constructed, and how organizations coordinate. Understanding this requires dismantling several interlocking assumptions that the mantra quietly reinforces — beginning with a confusion about value itself that runs beneath nearly all of them.

### Economic value versus intrinsic value

A foundational error in most discussions of AI and work is the conflation of intrinsic value with economic value. These are fundamentally different things, and confusing them leads to flawed reasoning about which human contributions will be rewarded in a restructured economy.

Intrinsic value is subjective and rooted in meaning. Air is vital to life and thus has infinite intrinsic value, yet because it is abundant, it commands no economic value in most circumstances — though compressed air for scuba diving does, because scarcity has been introduced. A soldier's family locket may be priceless to him but nearly worthless on the open market. Economic value only emerges when something meaningful also meets two conditions: scarcity of supply and relevance to demand. Economic value is what gets traded, requiring choices made under scarcity where preferences are revealed through willingness to exchange.

This distinction underpins what might be called the "human touch fallacy." When people claim that AI will never replace the human touch, or that humans will always be more valuable than ever, they are correct about intrinsic value but wrong to assume this automatically translates into economic value. Caregiving, for example, is deeply human and emotionally rich — high in intrinsic value — yet in algorithmically managed labor markets, caregivers may be treated as interchangeable commodities whose warmth and empathy go unrewarded economically. An Uber driver's politeness doesn't determine their pay; the algorithm's matching logic does. It is not enough to be human in the age of AI. What matters is how scarce one's particular form of human contribution is, because scarcity is what converts value into economic value. Skills like curiosity, curation, and judgment command economic premiums precisely because they are rare, path-dependent, and difficult to commoditize — not simply because they are human.

This distinction between intrinsic and economic value is not a peripheral philosophical point. It is the mechanism through which every systemic dynamic described below actually operates. Productivity decouples from value capture because productivity without scarcity generates no economic surplus. Jobs dissolve not because they stop being meaningful but because the coordination problem that made them economically scarce disappears. Skill premiums erode not because expertise loses its intrinsic worth but because AI makes it abundant. The intrinsic-economic gap is the fault line along which the restructuring of work proceeds.

### The task-level framing and its limits

The mantra rests on a binary: either AI performs your task for you (automation) or you perform it better with AI's help (augmentation). This frames the question as one of local optimization — doing the same work faster or more competently. But the economic value of any particular task depends on the system surrounding it. When the system changes, a task can become irrelevant even if no one has automated it — and even if it retains all its intrinsic worth.

Containerized shipping illustrates this precisely. Shipping containers did not simply automate the loading and unloading of cargo. They restructured the entire economic geography of global trade. Ports that had thrived under the old system — Liverpool, for instance — became marginal, while others like Singapore rose to dominance. Dockworkers were not replaced by cranes in any straightforward sense. The system in which their labor made sense was redesigned around a different logic of coordination, and their role ceased to exist within it. The task-level question ("Will a machine do my job?") entirely misses this kind of structural displacement.

### Productivity without value capture

A related assumption holds that if AI makes a worker more productive, that worker becomes more valuable. This is true only when the broader system of work remains stable — and, crucially, only when the productivity in question remains scarce. During periods of structural change, productivity and value capture decouple.

Fast fashion provides a concrete example. Factory productivity in garment manufacturing has increased enormously, but the economic surplus flows not to the workers or even the factory owners but to coordinating platforms like Shein that control distribution and demand aggregation. The productive capacity is commoditized; the scarce asset is coordination. This follows the logic of Ricardian rents: value accrues not to whoever produces the most but to whoever controls the scarcest resource in the system. When AI tools are widely available and easily replicated, the productivity they confer becomes table stakes rather than a competitive advantage. The surplus migrates to whoever controls the complementary asset that remains scarce — typically the ability to coordinate, distribute, or decide.

The intrinsic-economic distinction clarifies why this happens. A garment worker's skill and effort remain intrinsically valuable — the clothing still needs to be made, and made well. But because that productive capacity is abundant, its economic value collapses. The worker is not less skilled or less essential to the physical process. They are less scarce, and scarcity is the gate through which intrinsic value must pass to become economic value.

### Jobs as organizational artifacts

The mantra also assumes that jobs are stable units — fixed containers of tasks that might be augmented or automated but whose basic structure persists. In reality, jobs are artifacts of organizational design, constructed around specific coordination problems. When the coordination problem changes, the job can dissolve even if every individual task within it remains technically possible and personally meaningful.

The evolution of basketball positions offers an unexpectedly precise analogy. For decades, the sport was organized around five fixed positions — point guard, shooting guard, small forward, power forward, center — each with defined responsibilities. Analytics revealed that three-point shooting and floor spacing were far more efficient than the traditional inside-outside game, and the rigid positional structure gave way to fluid, capability-based roles. The center position was not automated. It was restructured out of relevance by a change in the system's logic. The question that matters is not "Will my job be automated?" but "Will the coordination problem that justifies this role still exist?"

### When the constraint disappears

The competitive framing — learn AI or lose to someone who has — assumes that the axis of competition remains stable. But technologies can eliminate the constraint that made a skill economically valuable in the first place, rendering competition along that axis meaningless.

Typists in the late twentieth century who learned word processing were not outcompeted by faster typists. The constraint that had justified their role — the high cost of producing and editing clean documents — was eliminated. Typing became a basic embedded skill rather than a specialized profession. The competitive race was real but irrelevant, because the environment had stopped rewarding the dimension along which they were competing. English longbowmen were not defeated by better archers but by gunpowder, which operated on entirely different principles. Recognizing which constraint makes a skill valuable, and detecting when that constraint is being removed, matters far more than racing to improve the skill itself.

There is a further subtlety. New workflows introduce new constraints that may have nothing to do with individual task performance. In relay racing, the fastest sprinters sometimes lose because they struggle with baton handoffs. Workflow-level coordination can matter more than individual speed, and AI-restructured workflows will create bottlenecks in places that current task-level thinking cannot anticipate.

### Workflow redesign, not workflow acceleration

The assumption that existing workflows will persist while AI merely accelerates them is among the most dangerous. Workflows are frequently reimagined entirely when new capabilities become available. Cricket's Decision Review System shifted adjudication from on-field umpires to television officials, changing not just who makes decisions but the sequence and rhythm of play itself. When workflows are redesigned, skills that were essential at one stage may not be needed at any stage — regardless of whether the worker has learned to use AI.

Organizations that focus on optimizing existing processes with AI risk what might be called optimizing into irrelevance: becoming maximally efficient at a workflow that the market or the technology is about to bypass entirely. The strategic question is not how to do the current thing faster but whether the current thing will still be the thing that needs doing.

### The politics of tools

AI is not a neutral instrument that passively serves its user. Tools carry embedded defaults and preferences that redistribute organizational power. Microsoft Excel shifted influence toward people who could model and simulate decisions, granting them disproportionate weight in organizational deliberation regardless of their formal authority. AI will do the same, restructuring who informs decisions and who makes them.

But AI introduces a further dimension that most prior tools did not: agentic execution. Unlike a spreadsheet, which presents options for a human to evaluate, an AI agent can make choices and allocate resources autonomously. This means AI does not merely assist a worker's contribution to a team — it can progressively substitute for parts of it, weakening the worker's organizational position even while nominally augmenting their output. Langdon Winner's concept of the politics of artifacts applies directly: design choices encode social consequences, often invisibly. Robert Moses's low parkway overpasses in New York excluded buses and the low-income populations who depended on them — not through explicit policy but through the physical design of infrastructure. AI systems will encode analogous choices about who has access, who has authority, and whose judgment counts.

The human touch fallacy is especially pernicious here. An organization might retain human-facing roles — customer service agents, care coordinators, advisors — because their intrinsic value is obvious and emotionally resonant. But if the algorithmic infrastructure treats those roles as interchangeable and routes economic surplus elsewhere, the humans in them are valued in name only. The system acknowledges their intrinsic worth while structurally denying them economic worth.

### The erosion beneath stable titles

Retaining a job title does not guarantee that its economic value is preserved. Session musicians illustrate this with painful clarity. The explosion of digital streaming created more recording work than ever, but streaming economics drove per-play revenue toward zero, and royalty structures concentrated income among a handful of headline artists. Musicians have more work at rates that no longer sustain a livelihood, producing increasingly formulaic output to chase algorithmic attention.

AI tools that enable average-skilled workers to produce output at the level of previously high-skilled workers have a flattening effect, decoupling expertise from compensation. The skill premium erodes not because the skill disappears but because it ceases to be scarce — and it is scarcity, not quality or meaning, that sustains economic value. A session musician's craft retains its intrinsic worth; what collapses is the economic premium that scarcity once conferred. And there is a recursive dimension: the more a worker uses AI, the more they generate training signal for systems that can replicate what they do, progressively fragmenting their own role and further eroding the scarcity of their contribution. Economic indicators that measure employment continuity rather than compensation quality can mask this erosion entirely.

### The firm as a site of transformation

Most organizations treat AI adoption as an addition — bolting tools onto existing structures through integrations and pilot programs — without rethinking strategy, organizational design, or workflows. This assumes the firm is a stable container that can absorb a transformative technology without itself being reshaped. But when a technology changes how decisions are made and how coordination happens, it reorganizes the firm. Treating AI as a feature upgrade rather than a new operating system promises transformation without reorganization, which is a contradiction that cannot hold indefinitely.

### The systemic view

Taken together, these dynamics reveal that the real impact of AI operates at the level of systems. It restructures how work is organized, how value flows, how roles are defined, and how organizations coordinate. The critical questions are not about whether individual workers adopt AI tools but about how AI changes the architecture of work itself — which constraints it removes, which new ones it creates, where surplus accumulates, and whose judgment remains irreplaceable.

The intrinsic-economic distinction is the thread that runs through every layer of this restructuring. Systems do not reward what is meaningful; they reward what is scarce. Human contributions that are abundant — however deeply felt, however essential to the texture of social life — will be treated as commodities in markets shaped by algorithmic coordination. The contributions that retain economic value will be those that are rare, path-dependent, and resistant to replication: not humanness in general, but specific, hard-to-substitute forms of human judgment, curation, and sense-making. The mantra about someone using AI taking your job is not wrong so much as it is pointed in the wrong direction, encouraging individuals to optimize locally while the system reorganizes around them — and while the gap between what is valued and what is rewarded widens beneath their feet.

## See Also

- [[z20260315-008|The Transformation of the Software Engineering Role in the Age of AI Coding Agents]] — applies the systemic restructuring argument to a concrete professional domain, demonstrating how the engineering role is being reorganized around planning, orchestration, and judgment as implementation is commoditized
- [[z20260315-018|LLMs, Cheap Answers, and the Economics of Questions]] — grounds the value-migration dynamic in information economics, showing how the collapse of answer scarcity shifts economic value toward the ability to formulate consequential questions
